content.text: Agent Service Composition
Agent service composition is the practice of building large agentic capabilities by wiring together
smaller, focused agent services. Rather than one monolithic agent that handles everything, you deploy
specialist agents (search, code, writing, data analysis) as independent HTTP services, then compose
them through an orchestrator agent that decides which specialists to call and in what order.
This mirrors how microservices decompose a monolithic backend — with the added dimension that the
glue logic is itself an LLM.
Exposing Agents as HTTP Services
Each specialist agent is wrapped in a lightweight HTTP service. The orchestrator calls these
services as tools. The key contract: input is a natural-language task (plus structured parameters);
output is a structured JSON response the orchestrator can interpret and pass to the next agent.

# pip install fastapi uvicorn anthropic
# Each specialist runs as its own service: uvicorn search_agent:app --port 8001

from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from anthropic import Anthropic

app = FastAPI()
client = Anthropic()

class SearchRequest(BaseModel):
    query: str
    max_results: int = 5

class SearchResponse(BaseModel):
    results: list[dict]
    summary: str

@app.post("/search", response_model=SearchResponse)
async def search(req: SearchRequest) -> SearchResponse:
    """Specialist agent: retrieves and summarises search results."""
    # In a real agent this would call a search API + do RAG
    # Shown simplified for clarity
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=400,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Search for: {req.query}\nReturn top {req.max_results} key facts as JSON list of {{title, snippet}}."
        }]
    )
    import json
    try:
        results = json.loads(resp.content[0].text)
    except (json.JSONDecodeError, IndexError):
        results = [{"title": "Result", "snippet": resp.content[0].text}]

    return SearchResponse(
        results=results[:req.max_results],
        summary=f"Found {len(results)} results for '{req.query}'",
    )
Orchestrator Agent Calling Specialist Services
The orchestrator defines each specialist as a tool whose
execute
function makes an HTTP
call to the relevant service. This lets the model decide the right sequence and combination of
specialists without the orchestrator code needing to know the task upfront.
Parallel Composition with asyncio
When multiple specialist calls are independent, running them in parallel cuts wall-clock latency
dramatically. Detect independence by checking whether the orchestrator calls them in the same
turn (all tool_use blocks appear in the same response).
Service Discovery and Health Checks
In production, specialist service URLs are not hardcoded — they come from a registry or
environment variables and are verified with health checks before use.
Composition vs Monolith Trade-offs
Aspect
Monolithic Agent
Composed Service Mesh
Deployment
One service, one model
Multiple services, independent scaling
Context window
All tools in one context — can get crowded
Each specialist has its own focused context
Latency
Sequential tool calls
Independent calls can run in parallel
Failure handling
Any tool failure aborts the whole turn
A specialist failure affects only that capability
Model selection
One model for everything
Use cheap models for simple specialists, stronger for complex
Operational complexity
Low — one service to deploy
Higher — service discovery, health checks, inter-service auth
Common Pitfalls
No timeout on specialist calls.
A specialist agent can hang indefinitely. Always set
timeout=
on
httpx
calls. 30–120 seconds depending on the specialist's expected latency.
Passing entire specialist outputs back to orchestrator context.
A search agent returning 50KB of results fills the orchestrator's context. Have specialists summarise or truncate before returning.
No inter-service authentication.
In a multi-tenant system, specialists must verify the caller's identity just like any external API. Pass the tenant JWT or a service-to-service token in the
Authorization
header.
Assuming specialist availability.
Services can be down. The orchestrator should handle a specialist returning an error gracefully — report partial results rather than failing entirely.
Summary
Agent service composition breaks monolithic agents into specialist HTTP services, each with a
focused capability and its own model/compute budget. The orchestrator calls specialists as tools,
runs independent calls in parallel with asyncio, and synthesises the results. This pattern improves
scalability, allows model selection per specialisation, and makes each capability independently
testable. The trade-off is operational complexity: you need service discovery, health checks,
inter-service auth, and timeout handling that a single-agent deployment does not require.
